event: Saint Jerome in a Rocky Landscape
symbols: towering rock formations — the grandeur and severity of the wilderness retreat
small figure at the base — human insignificance before the vastness of creation and God
cave shelter — the hermit's dwelling, stripped of worldly comfort
distant village — the world Jerome has left behind for contemplative solitude
description: A dramatic landscape dominated by towering white and grey rock formations that rise steeply against a dark, stormy sky. At the lower right, a tiny figure of a hermit (Jerome) sits in a rocky grotto or cave entrance, barely visible against the immense scale of the cliffs. In the middle distance, nestled among the rocks, a small village with houses and a church is visible. Green vegetation clings to the rocks and fills the valleys between the formations. The landscape extends into a vast panorama with distant green fields and more cliffs.
theological_description: Saint Jerome (c. 347-420), the great biblical translator who produced the Latin Vulgate, is frequently depicted as a hermit in the wilderness, reflecting his years of ascetic retreat in the Syrian desert. While post-biblical, Jerome's dedication to Scripture has direct biblical resonance. His translation work embodied the conviction that 'all Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness' (2 Timothy 3:16). The vast rocky landscape in which the tiny figure meditates evokes the biblical wilderness tradition — Moses at Sinai, Elijah at Horeb, Jesus in the Judean desert. The Psalmist's words find visual expression: 'I lift up my eyes to the mountains — where does my help come from? My help comes from the Lord, the Maker of heaven and earth' (Psalm 121:1-2). Jerome's solitary retreat into such forbidding terrain reflects his famous declaration: 'Ignorance of Scripture is ignorance of Christ.' The cave dwelling echoes Elijah's cave on Mount Horeb (1 Kings 19:9), where God spoke not in earthquake or fire but in a still small voice.

scripture_refs.reason: Lifting eyes to the mountains for help from the Lord — the vast rocky landscape as setting for divine encounter
All Scripture is God-breathed — Jerome's life was dedicated to translating and studying the Word
Elijah in the cave on Mount Horeb — Jerome's cave retreat echoes this biblical pattern
